schema_note: Published tiers: A_VERIFIED_LOW (ordinal ranked), B_PROBABLY_LOW, C_OPAQUE, MODERATE, ON_TRACK. See docs/methodology.html.
legal_disclaimer: Every figure is an estimate of OBSERVABLE giving from the cited sources. Does not measure total giving. Subjects have been offered a right-of-reply; responses are published on individual profiles. Corrections welcome — see methodology page.

billionaires.wealth_source: Tesla (CEO, ~13% stake); SpaceX (founder, ~42% stake, private); xAI (founder, ~50% stake); X Corp. (owner); Neuralink; The Boring Company
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Musk is the canonical 'richest person alive who has given least relative to wealth' example. 0.06% lifetime giving rate (Forbes April 2026) is one of the lowest in the Forbes 400. Additional concerns: political giving ($288M+ in 2024) exceeds lifetime philanthropy; Musk Foundation missed IRS 5% minimum three consecutive years; majority of 'giving' goes to Musk-controlled entities (The Foundation school + Fidelity DAF); Giving Pledge signed 2012 remains unfulfilled at 50% target ($405B gap).

billionaires.wealth_source: Google / Alphabet co-founder (1998); holds ~6% of Alphabet (Class B supervoting + Class C). Also longevity startup investments (Calico funded through Alphabet, not personally philanthropic). Recent AI startup (Dynatomics, reported 2025).
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Page is the canonical Tier A example: 22+ years as billionaire, never signed Giving Pledge, foundation grants are 77-100% DAF-warehoused, direct-to-operating-charity dollars are a small fraction of wealth (<1%), tax-minimization behavior (Fiji, California exit to Miami) not matched by giving velocity. Even accepting foundation grants-out as fully charitable (charitable intent at the foundation level), Page deploys ~0.7% of NW lifetime — an order of magnitude below 10% norm. The DailyMail 0.03% headline is defensible under a strict 'direct to operating charity' numerator; the defensible-generous framing still lands below 1%. Either way: LOW.

foundations_active_names: The Carl Victor Page Memorial Foundation

billionaires.wealth_source: Walmart Inc. — only daughter of founder Sam Walton. Walton family collectively owns ~45% of Walmart through Walton Enterprises LLC and Walton Family Holdings Trust. Wealth held primarily via trust structures rather than direct equity. Also founder/principal of Crystal Bridges Museum of American Art, Art Bridges Foundation, Heartland Whole Health Institute, and Alice L. Walton School of Medicine.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Alice Walton as 'the most publicly-visible Walton philanthropist — substantial and recognizable cultural + medical + museum-access giving, with zero officer compensation at her personal vehicles — BUT the foundations are accumulating assets faster than they deploy them (1.1% payout at ALWF 2024 is the signature red flag), and the $134B net worth is compounding faster than the giving. Forbes' own methodology places her in the 1%-4.99% lifetime-giving band. Observable ~$2.2B over 6 years is 0.27%/yr of current NW and 82% of the 10% formal norm. She has NOT signed the Giving Pledge (by family consensus) so cannot be in breach. Comparable reference class: other non-signers with similar wealth — where she looks relatively generous vs. Rob (more observable giving; less political giving) but still well short of the 10% formal norm.

foundations_active_names: Alice L. Walton School of Medicine (AWSOM)
Crystal Bridges Museum of American Art

billionaires.wealth_source: Koch Industries (chairman, co-CEO, majority owner after buying out David Koch estate and other siblings). Privately held — oil refining, chemicals, pipelines, paper, fertilizer, commodity trading. Also significant private-equity and real-estate holdings via Koch Industries.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Koch as the canonical political-substitution case. His 501(c)(3) foundation giving is real and tracked. But $5.3B to c4 political advocacy is ~5x all his lifetime charitable giving — a deliberate structural choice. Unlike Ellison (signed Giving Pledge, unfulfilled), Koch never promised and his legacy plan is explicitly c4-centric. Published-list classification MUST distinguish c3 giving from c4 transfers or it will misrepresent his philanthropic profile by 5-10x.

foundations_active_names: Charles J And Elizabeth R Koch Tr Charitable Private Foundation
Stand Together Fellowship (formerly Charles Koch Institute)

billionaires.wealth_source: Amazon.com founder (~8.6% stake after post-2019 divorce split with MacKenzie Scott); Blue Origin (100% private); Washington Post (100% private via Nash Holdings)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Bezos is the canonical example of 'biggest pledge in philanthropic history, behind schedule to deliver.' $2.3B Earth Fund disbursed through 2026 against $10B by 2030 target; $850M Day 1 Fund through 2025 against open-ended $2B; Smithsonian $200M fulfilled. His challenge is not whether he gives — he demonstrably does — but whether the pace matches the pledge and the wealth.

billionaires.wealth_source: Nvidia Corp. co-founder and CEO since 1993; ~3% ownership stake in Nvidia (world's most valuable company as of 2026). Wealth is concentrated in Nvidia stock held in his name, in a family trust, and via the Huang Foundation + GeForce Fund DAF.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Huang as a 'recently-minted mega-billionaire whose giving infrastructure has not scaled to match his wealth.' The foundation is actively ramping ($126M in 2024, required ~$369M in 2025). But structural issues remain: 65% of foundation payout goes to his own DAF, not to operating charities; GeForce Fund grantees are not disclosed; LOW_PAYOUT flag is active. Under economic-tenure (3yr) analysis he looks better (ratio 1.4x expected); under formal-tenure analysis he looks worse (0.47x expected). Both views are legitimate. Do NOT classify as 'unfulfilled pledge' — he never signed the Giving Pledge.

foundations_active_names: The Jen-Hsun and Lori Huang Foundation

billionaires.wealth_source: Walmart Inc. — youngest surviving son of founder Sam Walton. Walton family collectively owns ~45% of Walmart. Jim's primary operational vehicle is Arvest Bank (44% personal stake, ~$26B assets, ~$2.3B book value Dec 2025 FDIC filing); served as Arvest CEO until 2024, now Chairman of Arvest Bank Group. Walmart board director 2005-present (joined after brother John's 2005 death). Chairs Community Publishers Inc. (newspaper firm).
billionaires.tier_raw: Probably_Low (Tier B) — trending toward Tier A Verified Low
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Jim Walton as the canonical example of 'the least visible Walton heir' — a centibillionaire whose primary life's work has been a for-profit regional bank (Arvest), whose personal philanthropic vehicle (Town Branch Foundation, ~$500M endowment, no website) is dwarfed by his brother Rob's RMWF and his sister Alice's Crystal Bridges/AWF. He has not signed the Giving Pledge (by family position) so cannot be 'in breach.' His 2019 $1.2B Walmart-stock gift is the single biggest public philanthropic marker on his record, but the recipient was never disclosed and is most plausibly WFF — making it a transfer-in, not an out-the-door act of generosity to a specific cause. If Jim's goal were to deploy wealth for public good at scale, the mechanisms exist (TBF could grow 10x in a year from a single Walmart-stock contribution); they have not been used.

billionaires.wealth_source: Founder and CEO of Citadel LLC (hedge fund, ~$69B AUM) and Founder/Non-Executive Chairman of Citadel Securities (market maker). Both privately held.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Griffin is a useful contrast to Ellison: HIGH observable dollars, but HIGH political dollars and explicit refusal to sign a percentage pledge. On the Scrooge List frame he is NOT a canonical 'Scrooge' — he gives real money — but his score is pulled up by political substitution and the opacity of his DAF-channel giving.

foundations_active_names: Citadel Group Foundation (a.k.a. The Citadel Foundation)

billionaires.wealth_source: Oracle Corp. co-founder; 41% ownership stake; also significant Tesla holdings and Lawrence Investments family office
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Ellison as the canonical example of 'signed the Giving Pledge, hasn't followed through yet.' Observable giving ~$467M is 0.2% of current net worth and 8% of the 10% formal norm. The trust could still make good; as of 2026-04, it has not.

billionaires.wealth_source: Walmart Inc. — eldest son of founder Sam Walton; Walmart chairman 1992-2015, chairman emeritus thereafter. Walton family collectively owns ~45% of Walmart. Also principal owner of Denver Broncos (2022 purchase for $4.65B), 25.5% stake in Arvest Bank (~$2.3B book).
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Rob Walton as the canonical example of 'old-money dynasty — substantial joint-family foundation giving, no Giving Pledge signature, surging political giving, and individual wealth compounding faster than individual generosity.' Observable giving ~$1.5B over 6 years is 0.18%/yr of current NW and 53% of the 10% formal norm. He has NOT signed the Giving Pledge (by choice) so cannot be 'in breach' of it — the comparable reference class is other non-signers with similar wealth and similar observable giving rates.

billionaires.wealth_source: Alphabet / Google co-founder; large Class B and Class C Alphabet stake; Bayshore Global Management (family office) manages non-Alphabet holdings including Tesla stock
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Brin is the canonical case of 'real giving but still small relative to the pile.' Observable ~$2.4B is ~1% of current net worth and ~22% of the 10% formal norm. His Parkinson's commitment is genuine and historically significant — he is, factually, the largest individual donor to Parkinson's research ever. But that commitment now represents a tiny fraction of his wealth. Trajectory matters: if 2025's $2B in stock transfers disburses on 2024's $722M/yr cadence, his ratio will improve meaningfully in coming years.

billionaires.wealth_source: Blackstone Inc. co-founder, chairman & CEO; owns ~19% of publicly-traded Blackstone; also large annual compensation/dividend income ($1.2B in 2025)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Schwarzman is the canonical 'named gift' philanthropist. Observable $1.05B is ~2% of current net worth and 55% of the 10% formal norm. He has given substantially more than Ellison or Zuckerberg observably but is pursuing the classical posthumous-foundation strategy Buffett critiqued. Political giving ($78M+ since 2020) approaches philanthropic giving on an annual basis.

billionaires.wealth_source: Microsoft — joined 1980 as employee #30, granted 8% equity stake by Bill Gates; CEO 2000-2014; retains ~4% ownership (~333M shares). Also LA Clippers (purchased 2014 for $2B; Sportico valuation ~$6.72B Oct 2025) and The Forum.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Ballmer is the canonical 'probably giving a lot, but we can't independently verify' case. On self-reported + Forbes-reconciled figures, he is giving at 70-80% of the 10% formal norm — among the more generous top-15 billionaires. But the LLC structure means that if self-reporting stopped tomorrow, we would have very little ability to reconstruct the giving from public records. Readers should see Ballmer as 'transparency mode choice' — a billionaire who gives meaningfully but has chosen a vehicle class that lets him stop disclosing whenever he wants.

billionaires.wealth_source: Meta Platforms co-founder & CEO; ~13% ownership stake (as of Nov 2025 Bloomberg filing); also Zuckerberg private investments / family office
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Zuckerberg is the canonical LLC_OPACITY case. His 990-PF grants alone (~$1.87B 2017-2024) would place him mid-tier. The 99% Meta shares pledge, if fully disbursed to 501(c)(3)s over his lifetime, would make him one of the largest philanthropists in history. As of 2026-04, only ~2.3% of that notional has reached the 501(c)(3) arm of CZI. The rest sits in a for-profit LLC whose grantees we cannot independently verify. Readers should understand this as: 'Signed the Giving Pledge, structured primary giving vehicle as a for-profit LLC, self-reports large aggregate giving — external verification confirms less than 30% of the self-report.'

billionaires.wealth_source: Facebook (Meta) co-founder; Asana co-founder and former CEO; residual Meta holdings plus Asana equity drive net worth
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Moskovitz/Tuna as the canonical example of 'signed the Giving Pledge young and actually delivered.' Cumulative $5B in observable grants + $7.94B committed inside a transparent foundation + explicit spend-down intent + ~$10B already moved out of personal balance sheet into the philanthropic orbit. At $10.2B current personal net worth, they have given away or committed roughly double their Forbes personal net worth to charity already.

billionaires.name_legal: MacKenzie Scott (formerly MacKenzie Bezos; formerly MacKenzie Tuttle)
billionaires.wealth_source: Amazon divorce settlement 2019 — received ~25% of the couple's Amazon shares (~4% of Amazon outstanding, ~19.7M shares at settlement), tax-free under IRC §1041. No operating-company role. Author. Post-divorce wealth managed through family office Lost Horse LLC (Seattle, est. late 2019).
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Scott is the canonical methodological edge case for the Scrooge List: she is simultaneously one of the MOST observed (detailed grant-recipient database) and one of the MOST opaque (no foundation, undated DAF inflows, family-office LLC) billionaires in the US cohort. Her exclusion from Chronicle of Philanthropy's Philanthropy 50 (2024, 2025, 2026) despite giving more than the list's #1 ranked donor in 2025 ($7.1B vs. Bloomberg's $4.3B) is, by itself, evidence that standard philanthropy-tracking infrastructure is broken. The scrooge-list methodology choice to credit Scott's self-published grants-to-501(c)(3) totals is a deliberate departure from Chronicle methodology and should be flagged in methodology prose.

billionaires.wealth_source: Bloomberg L.P. (financial data/media terminal, founded 1981) — sole owner of ~88% of the company; also former 3-term mayor of New York City
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Bloomberg is the reference point for 'what a centibillionaire actively fulfilling the pledge looks like' — high base rate of disclosed giving (~$3.7B/yr, $21.1B cumulative), structural Bloomberg L.P. succession commitment, clean foundation governance (12%+ payout, nominal comp), no red flags. His observable/pledge-target ratio (~19%) is ~95× Ellison's (~0.2%). Use Bloomberg to calibrate the upper boundary of the Scrooge tier system. Any scoring formula that classifies Bloomberg as 'Scrooge-like' is prima facie miscalibrated.

foundations_active_names: Bloomberg Family Foundation Inc. (dba Bloomberg Philanthropies)

billionaires.wealth_source: Dell Technologies founder/CEO (~45-50% stake); also Broadcom stake (from 2023 VMware sale to Broadcom for $68B); DFO Management / MSD Capital family office; Secureworks stake
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Dell as the anti-Ellison: did NOT sign Giving Pledge but HAS built a serious working foundation over 25 years, zero-comp principals, broad operations. Key tensions: (1) $5B endowment injection accumulating faster than deploying (low payout ratio watch); (2) $210M DAF transfer opacity; (3) $6.25B Trump Accounts pledge is NOT conventional charity. Dell is notable for doing real philanthropy without the public commitment apparatus (Pledge, LLC, splashy vehicles).

billionaires.wealth_source: Nike, Inc. co-founder (1964, originally Blue Ribbon Sports); largest shareholder and chairman emeritus. Also holds Laika (stop-motion animation studio, owned via son Travis Knight) and other private investments.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Knight as the counterexample to Ellison: no Giving Pledge signature, but substantially MORE observable giving delivered ($3.28B vs $0.47B) at a much smaller net worth ($35B vs $227B). Knight demonstrates that pledge-signing is neither necessary (he hasn't signed) nor sufficient (Ellison has signed, minimally delivered). On-track classification should be qualified by: (1) foundation payout sitting at the 5% legal minimum, and (2) the $2B OHSU pledge is a multi-year commitment at age 88.

foundations_active_names: Knight Foundation (Phil & Penny Knight private foundation, Beaverton OR)

billionaires.wealth_source: Bridgewater Associates — founder 1975, world's largest hedge fund at peak (~$124B AUM). Sold his last shares of Bridgewater in July 2025 for ~$6.8B cash. Remaining wealth is diversified across cash, family office holdings, and real estate.
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Readers should see Dalio as a signed Giving Pledge member who is materially delivering on the commitment. Key nuances: (1) the $7B+ 'Dalio Philanthropies' lifetime figure includes transfers INTO the foundations and family fund, not just grants OUT — actual charitable deployment is lower. (2) LOW_PAYOUT flag on Dalio Foundation Inc 2021-2024 is real — the foundation has ballooned from $740M to $1.67B in assets while grant cadence slowed. (3) Connecticut partnership governance collapse in 2020 is a genuine governance flag. (4) Observable giving is understated in this file because Dalio Family Fund Inc 990s are not yet retrieved.

billionaires.wealth_source: Berkshire Hathaway — chairman, CEO (stepping down end of 2025) and largest individual shareholder; essentially 100% Berkshire common stock at time of 2006 pledge (98%+ of net worth)
billionaires.tier_reasoning: Use Buffett as the anchor/ceiling in lists and methodology pages: 'Buffett's $60B+ lifetime giving, with 99.5% of remainder committed at death, is the reference standard by which we judge other pledge signers.' Ellison gap to Buffett-equivalent = 99.8% short. A billionaire's position on the Scrooge list is effectively measured by distance from Buffett.
